text: Hi friends, this video demonstrates our technique of performing a TEP to peripheral inguinal hernia. This is an elderly gentleman with a bilateral inguinal hernia.

The surgeon insufflates carbon dioxide in the preperitoneal plane, revealing the white posterior rectus sheath. The cobweb-like spaces open up as the avascular plane is developed, with care taken to stay above the shiny posterior sheath.
The surgeon performs telescopic dissection to develop the pre-peritoneal space, revealing a small branch from the inferior epigastric vessel.

The surgeon identifies the white, shiny posterior rectus sheath and carefully dissects close to it, ensuring that the inferior epigastric vessels remain attached to the anterior abdominal wall. This technique prevents the vessels from being displaced downward, which would complicate the dissection process.

The surgeon identifies the anatomical planes under vision, continuing the dissection in the avascular space. The rectus muscle is not left bare, with a small fascia covering it. The posterior rectus sheath is visible, but becomes deficient three to four centimeters below the umbilicus, where the thin fascia makes the peritoneum prone to puncture.
The surgeon carefully identifies the rectus muscle and ensures a small fascia is left covering it. The dissection continues between the two layers of the transversalis fascia, avoiding undue injury.

The surgeon uses controlled forward and backward movements with the telescope to navigate the preperitoneal space, minimizing sweeping motions to reduce the risk of iatrogenic injury to the peritoneum during the early stage of the hernia surgery.
The surgeon reaches the pubic bone and begins dissection at the midline, gradually moving medially towards the opposite side to create a wider space for dissection.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection using gentle movements, avoiding undue pressure to prevent peritoneal defects and maintain pneumoperitoneum.

The key to perform a good TEP is a very good understanding of the anatomy and attention to small details. Because these small finer details will enable us to perform the surgery without much of a trouble.
The surgeon develops the medial retropubic space of Ridges and the lateral space of Bogros during the TEP hernia repair, preparing for the dissection of the hernia sac.

peritoneum it doesn't really sorry loss of pneumo it doesn't really affect our progress in the surgery because the spaces are well developed we can still manage to complete the surgery even without any need for addition of an additional port or a various needle into the abdomen to remove the pneumo peritoneum which is going and created
The surgeon identifies a direct hernia, noting the absence of the cupous ligament. Just lateral to this area, the surgeon enters an avascular plane and gradually expands it.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the inferior epigastric vessels, which are now visible, using controlled force to separate the fascia and develop the lateral space.
The surgeon carefully dissects the plane, revealing the edge of the peritoneum just below the inferior epigastric vessels. The dissection progresses higher to further identify anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon identifies the arcuate ligament, clearly visible in the surgical field. The fossa is seen going anterior and becoming fused. The surgeon pushes the vessel laterally to facilitate the insertion of a working port, aiming for optimal triangulation.
The surgeon introduces a small needle to verify the angle and ensure the peritoneum is not being injured. Subsequently, the port is inserted under direct vision.

The surgeon uses a low-profile hook instrument with low monopolar wattage to carefully release and dissect thick fibrous strands or adhesions within the avascular plane, minimizing the generation of fumes.

The surgeon navigates through the avascular plane, which is visibly expanding due to the insufflation of carbon dioxide at a pressure of twelve millimeters. The tissues are being carefully appreciated as the plane opens up, allowing for precise dissection.

The surgeon identifies a vessel that is part of the corona mortis, located on the cupous ligament between the external iliac vein and the obturator vein. Using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, the surgeon carefully dissects around the vessel to maintain clear visibility and avoid bleeding.

The surgeon observes that the rectus muscle appears compromised inferiorly, indicating an anterior approach. The fascia is thin in this patient, making it crucial to maintain the correct plane during dissection.

The surgeon uses a single-hand instrument to create space on the opposite side of the bilateral hernia under direct vision. The working port is introduced to achieve triangulation for effective mesh fixation.
The surgeon meticulously crosses the midline using short bursts of cautery combined with blunt dissection, preparing to anchor the mesh to the cupous ligament without mechanical fixation.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide insufflation to open avascular planes, providing clear visualization for gradual dissection towards the opposite side. Good traction is applied to avoid bleeding or trauma to important structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects the fibrotic tissue using short bursts of cautery, ensuring the inferior epigastric vessels remain in the roof and are not inadvertently dissected to the floor.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery and blunt dissection to open up the tissue planes, pushing the peritoneum down and expanding the lateral space. The dissection progresses towards the lateral border of the left rectus muscle, with the inferior epigastric vessels soon becoming visible.

The surgeon adjusts the plane of dissection to ensure the inferior epigastric vessels remain on the roof, preserving a small fascia on the muscle. The dissection continues just below this fascia to prevent the vessels from being brought to the floor.

The surgeon performs further dissection, revealing fibrotic tissue between two fascia layers, likely due to previous episodes of irreducibility or strangulation. The fibrotic tissue is gradually dissected away using sharp dissection with a low monopolar cautery, clearly exposing the avascular plane between the fascia layers.

The surgeon identifies a clear potential space in the midline, where fibrous bands are present. These bands are dissected to separate the bladder from the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring adequate space for mesh placement without risk of rolling.

The surgeon encounters fibrotic strands adjacent and below the pubic symphysis, which are more pronounced compared to the right side. Using sharp dissection, the fibrotic attachments are taken down, revealing the pubic symphysis and the arch of the bone clearly.

The pubic symphysis is visible as the surgeon performs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to expose the area. The dissection reveals the cupus ligament, a white shiny structure, which is clearly visible and emits a bright white light known as the 'white house sign.'

The surgeon dissects the remaining fibrous attachments on the right side of the corpus ligament, revealing the shiny 'lighthouse sign' as the tissues are pushed away. The corpus ligament is fully exposed, reflecting light brightly, while the bone appears less luminous. The dissection extends three to four centimeters below the pubic arch to ensure the mesh will seat comfortably without rolling.

The surgeon meticulously dissects remaining fibrotic attachments, revealing the absence of a direct hernia on this side. The dissection continues to open up the lateral space, with the inferior epigastric vessels visible and the surgeon maintaining close proximity to these vessels.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to open up the potential lateral space, carefully identifying the white tissue planes. The confined space is prone to fogging, so monopolar energy is kept at low wattage to manage small vessel coagulation without excessive fogging. The lateral space is rapidly enlarged.
The surgeon releases the midline attachment, making the dissection easier. A small vessel is identified, and careful dissection is performed on the right plane to avoid trauma to the peritoneum.
The surgeon uses a sharp C-monopolar hook to take down fibrotic strands, coagulating and further dissecting the area. The fibrotic tissue is unusually extensive, requiring careful manipulation.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum, which is fused in some areas, and uses a combination of energy to separate the tissues. Short bursts of energy are applied under direct vision to release the structures, continuing the dissection of the peritoneum.

The surgeon continues to open up the lateral space, inserting both working instruments at or just below the umbilicus, along the lateral border of the rectus. The dissection is performed carefully, staying medial to the inferior epigastric vessels to avoid trauma and bleeding. The camera port is positioned to ensure the upper edge of the mesh is placed correctly.

The surgeon dissects loose areolar tissue against the muscle, ensuring no crumpling effect occurs. The arcuate ligament is clearly visible on the left side. A needle is introduced to identify the direction and orientation of the structures.

A 5mm working port is inserted on the left side, with its tip clearly visible. The surgeon uses the assisting port to further dissect the lateral space of Bogros. The instrument provides traction and counter-traction, allowing the tissue planes to open up smoothly. An atraumatic instrument in the surgeon's left hand acts like a surgical finger, applying pressure to identify tissue planes and occasionally grasping tissues.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum and the dulux fascia covering the nerves on the lateral side. The muscles appear intact, with fascia visible on top. The edge of the mesh is retracted, and dissection occurs between two avascular planes using traction and counter traction, without the need for cautery. The surgeon continues to dissect and release attachments, including the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon releases fibrotic attachments near the arc width line to ensure the lateral edge of the mesh is seated comfortably. Sharp dissection is performed close to the inferior epigastric vessels, taking care to avoid trauma and bleeding. The pubic bone and bladder are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects and pushes the thin fibrovascular fascia around a large indirect hernia sac, which is now clearly visible after creating the lateral space. The lateral peritoneum, previously dissected, is also visible.

The surgeon divides the transversalis fascia fibers to open up the anatomy, revealing the triangle of doom. The dissection focuses on the vas deferens and testicular vessels, ensuring both medial and lateral spaces are developed and kept open to prevent complications from small injuries to the peritoneum.

The surgeon identifies the pseudosac and begins reducing the hernia using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques. An atraumatic medline grasper is employed to grasp tissues while progressing the dissection, occasionally using cautery to aid in the process.

The surgeon begins the sac dissection, starting from lateral to medial, using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The edge of the pseudosac is visible and being pushed upwards. As the dissection progresses medially, the white shiny vas deferens becomes visible in the surgical field, along with the testicular vessels.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac, exposing it fully around the internal ring. The peritoneum can be divided at this level, with the option to apply an endo loop or ligature, leaving the distal sac intact.

The surgeon has skeletonized the entire hernia sac and is progressing medially, carefully avoiding the vas deferens. The vas deferens is now more visible, and the surgeon uses blunt dissection and minimal electrical energy to avoid damage to the cord.

The testicular vessels are visible in the surgical field. The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to carefully separate and push the testicular vessels and vas deferens away from the hernia sac.

The surgeon continues the gradual dissection of the hernia sac, ensuring it is completely isolated and free from surrounding tissues. The sac is carefully inspected to confirm it is empty before using cautery, opting for sharp and blunt dissection if there is any suspicion of contents.

The surgeon exposes the bluish peritoneum, indicating complete dissection around the hernia sac. Using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, the fascia is opened to hook the sac securely. The wide sac is visible, and care is taken to avoid trauma to the vas amidst fibrous tissue.

The surgeon isolates a large indirect hernia sac using a combination of short bursts of energy and blunt dissection, ensuring other structures are pushed away. The thin fascia allows for rapid progress as the surgeon opens the instrument to get around the sac, facilitating retraction and expansion.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the fascia between the hernia sac and the testicular vessels and vas. The opposite side is visible, indicating that the dissection has progressed across the area, allowing control over the vas and testicular vessels.
The surgeon follows the proximal sac distally, using short bursts of monopolar energy to push away small sling fibers, facilitating the identification and retraction of the sac's fundus.

The surgeon meticulously skeletonizes the hernia sac, ensuring no undue bleeding occurs during dissection. Vital structures such as the vas deferens and testicular vessels are kept well away to prevent injury. Transversalis sling fibers are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon continues to dissect around the hernia sac, ensuring that the testicular vessels are carefully pushed away from the dissection area to prevent injury. The dissection is performed circumferentially around the sac to achieve a thorough separation.

The surgeon controls a small bleeding vein using monopolar energy, ensuring the vas deferens and testicular vessels are kept away from the surgical field. The sac dissection continues, with adjustments made to the position on the thin peritoneum.

The surgeon uses sharp bursts of cautery to further dissect the hernia sac, ensuring the vas deferens and testicular vessels remain unharmed. The fundus of the sac becomes visible, and small fibrous attachments on top are carefully removed for a comprehensive dissection.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the hernia sac, approaching the end of the fundus, which is visually identifiable as a bulgy U-shaped structure. Minimal dissection remains to fully isolate the sac.

The surgeon carefully releases the final fibrous attachments at the fundus of the hernia sac, which is held in their hands, completing the sac dissection. The camera view is adjusted to enhance clarity, revealing small fibrous fasciae that are still present. The surgeon prepares to proceed with the parietalization of the cord structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the fibrous planes at the edge of the hernia sac, ensuring no undue rent occurs. The remaining attachments between the sac and cord structures are released, with the vas and testicular vessels safely pushed away. Short bursts of energy are used to coagulate and minimize bleeding during the dissection.

The surgeon releases the last of the fibrous strands, freeing the entire cord structures. The camera view is adjusted for better visualization, ensuring the final attachments are taken down with precision.

The surgeon releases the last small fibrous strands between the fundus, completing the dissection of the sac. The sac is visibly free and descends into the abdominal area due to carbon dioxide insufflation. No injury to the peritoneum is observed, indicating a successful dissection.

The surgeon continues dissection to clearly identify and mobilize the edge of the peritoneum, ensuring it is pushed away to allow comfortable seating of the mesh. The lateral space is widened, with care taken to avoid injury to the peritoneum while dividing small fascia.

The surgical field is obscured by smoke, causing the camera lens to fog. The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum as being safely away and proceeds to divide the transversalis fascia attachment, which forms the arcuate ligament.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to manipulate the pseudo sac, applying short bursts of energy to manage vascular areas. The periphery of the hernia defect is visible, and dissection occurs between the inferior epigastric vessel and the peritoneum to create lateral space.

The surgeon carefully dissects and pulls away loose areolar tissue near the inferior epigastric vessel, ensuring to stay in the correct plane to avoid injury to the peritoneum. The edge of the peritoneum becomes clearly visible during this process.
In the TAPP procedure, the surgeon uses blunt traction and counter traction near the vessel, carefully manipulating the tissue without concern for peritoneal injuries or gas leaks.

The edge of the mesh is visible alongside the vas deferens. The surgeon carefully identifies and preserves these structures while applying medial traction to divide the thin fascial tissues. This step is performed cautiously due to the proximity of the external iliac vein to the dissection area.

The surgeon applies medial traction to pull tissues away from the external iliac vein, which appears as a blue structure to the right of the instrument. The vein is collapsed due to the pneumoperitoneum, necessitating careful dissection to avoid injury.

The surgeon isolates and dissects the external iliac vein, visible as a blue tubular structure with venous pulsation. The external iliac artery, a white tubular structure, is located just lateral to the vein. The surgeon continues to dissect and open up the lateral space of Bogros.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum, applying traction without holding it unnecessarily. The Geolux fascia is preserved to prevent direct exposure of nerves to the mesh, reducing the risk of chronic pain.
The surgeon continues the dissection using blunt techniques, revealing avascular tissue planes. A noticeable bulge indicates a small dent on the peritoneum, but the procedure progresses smoothly.

The surgeon clearly identifies the white peritoneal edge and uses short bursts of energy to dissect the thin fascia away from it, ensuring complete separation of the peritoneum. Blunt dissection is also employed to facilitate this process.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to prevent oozing, pushing the vas deferens away to ensure no structures are damaged. The vas deferens is visibly retracted, and short bursts of energy are applied to further dissect the peritoneum while keeping the surface area small by opening the instrument's jaws.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to move cartilaginous structures away from the area of dissection. The peritoneum is further dissected, with the surgeon staying close to it in an avascular field, minimizing the risk of damaging surrounding structures.

The surgeon continues with a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to mobilize the peritoneum, extending the dissection caudally until the card structures turn medially, crossing the obliterated umbilical artery or median umbilical ligament. The scope is cleaned to maintain a clear view.

The external iliac vein is visible, showing pulsation and appearing collapsed due to carbon dioxide insufflation. The surgeon continues lateral dissection, revealing the edge of the peritoneum and a small defect below the instrument, which is grasped for further manipulation.

In the left hypochondrial region at Palmer's point, the surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum using an open instrument technique to manage gas leakage. The peritoneum is grasped and carefully dissected away, revealing the entire edge of the peritoneum clearly.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the peritoneum, moving away from the area of the defect. The defect is moderate in size, and an endo loop will be applied to secure it.

Applying a clip is something a lot of people do, but I feel that more than a clip, an endo loop would be a better option when we are trying to secure. When that is also not possible, we can always do a suturing of the peritoneal defect.

The surgeon uses a pursing suture technique to obliterate the peritoneal defect, ensuring the mesh is completely extraperitonealized to prevent direct contact with the viscera. The peritoneum is well mobilized on the lateral side, with further mobilization possible if needed.
The surgeon releases the peritoneum from the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring the mesh is seated properly to prevent rolling and shrinkage. The dissection proceeds smoothly through avascular planes.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to gradually release the peritoneum from the anterior abdominal wall, widening the lateral space. The transversalis fascia is also released, allowing for the placement of a large mesh without crumbling or rolling. The lateral space appears wide and well-dissected.

The surgeon coagulates a small area of bleeding using electrocautery, controlling the bleeding effectively. The peritoneum is further pushed away, revealing the vas deferens as it begins to turn medially.

The surgeon completes the dissection as the vas deferens turns medially, indicating the endpoint for peritoneal mobilization. This ensures adequate space for a 12-centimeter mesh to be comfortably seated without further mobilization.

The minimum recommended nowadays is fifteen into twelve centimeter. So I generally prefer to use a low weight polypropylene mesh. for the reasons that the foreign body reaction is definitely less and also the shrinkage is comparatively less compared to a heavy weight mesh. Even though there is no statistically significant differences between the two meshes, definitely the handling of the mesh
The surgeon observes the vas turning medial, marking the limit for peritoneal dissection. The external iliac vein is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the phase where a potential obturator hernia can present, noting the absence of fat in this area. The surgical field is opened up to visualize this space.
The surgeon meticulously dissects the facial layer to expose the obturator vessels and obturator nerve. The obturator, a white structure, is visible alongside the coronal motors near the loop.
The surgeon identifies the peritoneal defect, clearly visible in the surgical field. An instrument is used to grasp the defect, and an endo loop is placed around it.

The surgeon uses an endo loop to push the hernia sac and release the instrument into the loop, pulling the peritoneum to apply the loop comfortably. The loop is tightened, and the instrument is released, effectively obscuring the peritoneal defect.

If you still have a doubt during the initial phases, we can always put in a framework camera into the abdomen after the hernia to make sure that there are no undue rents. If you identify a rent which you feel is quite large, you can always put in the two additional coats into the abdomen as well and suture the defects.

But it is very important to know that you have to close all the defects to make sure that the mesh is totally extraperitoneal and you do not have a potential for it to cause any trouble by coming in contact with the viscera.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to further dissect the tissues and hernia sac, creating an adequate space for the mesh placement. The peritoneum is pushed downwards, revealing a wide lateral space for the mesh to sit securely.
The surgeon displays a large, wide space that has been dissected, providing ample room for mesh placement.

The surgeon further dissects the peritoneum on the left side to identify the vas deferens, which is seen turning medially. The external iliac artery is pointed out with a hook, showing visible pulsation. The dissection mirrors the right side, creating space for the mesh placement.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument to meticulously dissect adjacent to the edge of the peritoneum, clearly visible and pushed away to avoid trauma. The dissection progresses from the lateral space towards the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring the entire area is carefully separated.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum and continues to create an adequate lateral space for the mesh placement during the transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) hernia repair. The peritoneum is carefully dissected to ensure the mesh can sit comfortably without risk of recurrence.
The video shows the dissected area on the left side, revealing an indirect hernia without a visible sac, indicating no potential for seroma formation.

The surgeon identifies the testicular vessels and the vas deferens positioned medially, with the triangle of doom located between these structures. The external iliac artery is visible within the triangle of doom, requiring careful manipulation. The inferior epigastric vessel is also visible, along with the widened internal ring through which the indirect hernia was present.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric artery, located medial to the indirect inguinal hernia. The pubic bone and pubic arch are visible, along with the symphysis.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of the instrument pointing to a small direct hernia, highlighting the hernia defect. The dissection extends well below the required three to four centimeters for optimal mesh placement.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessel lateral to the hernia, indicating a direct inguinal hernia through Hesselbach's triangle. The vessel is clearly visible and pointed out with a surgical hook.
The surgeon points to the hernial defect, highlighting the external iliac vein and artery with the left instrument. The vas deferens is visible, turning medially near the external iliac artery.

The surgeon points to the external iliac vein, which appears collapsed due to carbon dioxide insufflation. The vas deferens is visible on the right side, entering the indirect hernia sac. The testicular vessels are also shown, forming the triangle of doom along with the reflected peritoneum.

The surgeon handles a lightweight polypropylene mesh, rolling two-thirds of it while leaving one-third open. A suture is placed in the middle of the rolled portion to facilitate easier handling. The mesh is rapidly positioned within the surgical field.

A 15x13 cm mesh with curved cuts is positioned, accommodating the psoas muscle laterally and the prostate medially. The mesh overlaps approximately four centimeters beyond the midline, ensuring a minimum two-centimeter overlap beyond the pubic symphysis, as visible in the small-built patient.
The surgeon ensures the mesh is firmly positioned against the abdominal wall, preventing any coiling or rolling up, maintaining its intended placement.
The surgeon uses a proline suture to take a bite on the white, shiny Cooper's ligament, which is being carefully identified in the surgical field.

The surgeon places a suture in the Cooper's ligament during the TEP hernia repair, demonstrating the superficial bite taken with the needle. The needle remains firm in position, indicating a secure bite on the ligament without excessive depth.
The surgeon knots the proline suture loosely to secure the lightweight polypropylene mesh in position, ensuring it remains stable without excessive tightening.

The surgeon places loose knots, similar to an add knot, to secure the lightweight mesh in position, preventing migration. The mesh is positioned in a closed space, which is particularly important for thin patients to avoid the sensation of mesh prolapse or pseudo hernia.
The surgeon cuts the preformed knot and removes the suture, then proceeds to unroll the remaining two-thirds of the mesh against the abdominal wall.

The surgeon unrolls the last section of the mesh along the upper edge, ensuring it seats comfortably against the abdominal wall. The dissection extends cranially to facilitate proper mesh placement in the surgical field.

The mesh is fully seated against the anterior abdominal wall, positioned securely without any issues. The surgeon holds the mesh in place for a few seconds to ensure it remains stable, indicating that the dissection was performed adequately.
The surgeon places the mesh on the right side, ensuring it is well dissected and positioned. A bite is taken through the Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh in place.

The surgeon stretches the ligament while taking a suture bite, with the needle remaining in place. The mesh is deployed similarly to the opposite side, showing both meshes overlapping effectively. The dissection below the pubic area is visible.
